stem:
A 24-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of bipolar I disorder is brought to the emergency department by her family for being mute, immobile in unusual postures, and not eating or drinking for 36 hours. Her mother says she stopped responding two days ago after stopping her lithium and quetiapine three weeks ago to start a master's degree.

On examination she stares ahead, follows commands occasionally, holds her arm wherever you place it for over 30 seconds, and grimaces. There are no focal neurological signs. She is afebrile. Family report no recent infection, no head injury, no street drug use, and no withdrawal symptoms. There is no rigidity, no autonomic instability, and no fever to suggest neuroleptic malignant syndrome.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 88 /min, blood pressure 122/76 mmHg, respiratory rate 14 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale: stupor, mutism, waxy flexibility, posturing, grimacing; no rigidity, no clonus, no fever
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, CK 220 U/L (40 to 240), TSH 1.6 mIU/L, glucose 5.0 mmol/L; CT head non-contrast normal; urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Order an immediate lumbar puncture to exclude autoimmune encephalitis
options.rationale: Autoimmune encephalitis is on the differential but a primary psychiatric catatonia with a clear precipitant (medication cessation) and normal CT and inflammatory markers does not mandate LP before the lorazepam challenge. LP is considered if challenge fails or red flags emerge. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Statement)

options.text: Commence haloperidol 5 mg IM now to treat the underlying psychosis
options.rationale: Antipsychotics, particularly typical agents, can worsen catatonia and precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome. They are avoided until catatonia is broken with benzodiazepines or ECT. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Statement)

options.text: Restart lithium at the prior maintenance dose to reverse the mood episode driver
options.rationale: Restarting lithium is appropriate as a longer-term plan but does not address the acute catatonic state. Catatonia carries risks of dehydration, deep vein thrombosis, and aspiration that need same-day intervention. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Statement)

options.text: Trial IM or IV lorazepam 1 to 2 mg with reassessment of catatonia scale at 30 minutes
options.rationale: A lorazepam challenge is the standard diagnostic and initial therapeutic test for catatonia. An improvement of 50% or more on the Bush-Francis scale within 30 to 60 minutes confirms benzodiazepine-responsive catatonia and directs further treatment with scheduled lorazepam, often 6 to 24 mg per day in divided doses. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Statement)

options.text: Refer for urgent ECT before any pharmacological intervention is given
options.rationale: ECT is the next step if the lorazepam challenge fails or for malignant catatonia. Benzodiazepines come first as both diagnostic and therapeutic. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Statement)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a syndromic diagnosis recognised across primary psychiatric (mood, psychotic) and medical causes (autoimmune encephalitis, metabolic, infective). The Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale is the standard assessment. A lorazepam challenge of 1 to 2 mg IM or IV with reassessment in 30 to 60 minutes is both diagnostic and the first therapeutic step. Maintenance lorazepam (6 to 24 mg per day, occasionally higher) is then titrated. ECT is the second-line treatment for non-responders and the first-line for malignant catatonia (with autonomic instability and hyperthermia). Antipsychotics are avoided until catatonia has resolved.
key_points: Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IM or IV with reassessment at 30 to 60 minutes is the diagnostic challenge
Maintenance lorazepam 6 to 24 mg per day in divided doses for responders
Avoid antipsychotics until catatonia resolves; risk of NMS and worsening
ECT is second-line and first-line for malignant catatonia
explanation.pearls: Catatonia: lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IM or IV challenge, reassess Bush-Francis at 30 to 60 minutes. Responders go to 6 to 24 mg per day in divided doses. ECT is second-line and first-line for malignant catatonia. Avoid antipsychotics until resolution.

stem:
A 38-year-old man with major depressive disorder is brought to the emergency department by his housemate with restlessness, sweating, and tremor that began six hours ago. His housemate says he ran out of sertraline two weeks ago and was started on phenelzine three days ago by his private psychiatrist after multiple SSRI failures. He took his usual dose this morning then took two of his housemate's tramadol 50 mg tablets for a sore back.

On arrival he is agitated, has spontaneous clonus in both legs (sustained for several seconds), brisk deep tendon reflexes, ocular clonus, and diaphoresis. There is no muscle rigidity. His pupils are dilated.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 134 /min, blood pressure 168/96 mmHg, respiratory rate 26 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 38.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: agitated, spontaneous lower limb clonus, ocular clonus, hyperreflexia (4+) more pronounced in lower limbs, dilated pupils, diaphoresis, no lead-pipe rigidity
data_table.Investigations: CK 380 U/L (40 to 240); UEC normal; ABG within normal limits; ECG sinus tachycardia QTc 430 ms; venous lactate 2.1 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Serotonin syndrome from MAOI plus tramadol with incomplete SSRI washout
options.rationale: The Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria are met (spontaneous clonus, plus agitation, sweating, and hyperthermia). The combination of phenelzine (irreversible MAOI) plus tramadol (serotonergic) plus an inadequate sertraline washout (less than 2 weeks) is classic. Lower limb hyperreflexia, ocular clonus, and dilated pupils favour serotonin syndrome over NMS. (Source: Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome with autonomic instability and rigidity
options.rationale: NMS presents with lead-pipe rigidity, bradyreflexia, and an indolent onset over days, typically after antipsychotic initiation or dose increase. This patient has hyperreflexia and clonus and no antipsychotic exposure. (Source: Hunter Criteria; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Anticholinergic toxidrome from tramadol's noradrenergic and serotonergic effects
options.rationale: Anticholinergic toxicity features dry skin and mucous membranes, urinary retention, and a flushed face, not the diaphoresis and clonus here. Tramadol can have anticholinergic effects but the dominant picture is serotonergic. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Thyroid storm precipitated by recent infection and tramadol use
options.rationale: Thyroid storm causes hypermetabolic state but typically with goitre, atrial fibrillation, and bradyreflexia. The classic combination of agitation, spontaneous clonus, ocular clonus, and diaphoresis points to serotonin syndrome. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Sympathomimetic toxidrome from concurrent stimulant intoxication
options.rationale: Stimulant intoxication can produce agitation, hypertension, and tachycardia but does not classically produce lower-limb-dominant clonus or ocular clonus. The medication history is the discriminator. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Serotonin syndrome is diagnosed clinically using the Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria: in the setting of a serotonergic agent, the presence of spontaneous clonus, OR inducible clonus with agitation or diaphoresis, OR ocular clonus with agitation or diaphoresis, OR tremor and hyperreflexia, OR hypertonia plus temperature above 38 degrees Celsius with ocular or inducible clonus, is diagnostic. The pattern of hyperreflexia, lower-limb-predominant clonus, mydriasis, and acute onset distinguishes it from NMS (rigidity, bradyreflexia, days to develop). MAOI plus tramadol or other serotonergic agent is a particularly high-risk combination, requiring a 2-week SSRI washout before introducing an MAOI.
key_points: Hunter Criteria use spontaneous or inducible clonus, ocular clonus, hyperreflexia, agitation, diaphoresis, hyperthermia
Serotonin syndrome: hyperreflexia, lower-limb-dominant clonus, mydriasis, acute onset
NMS: lead-pipe rigidity, bradyreflexia, indolent onset, antipsychotic exposure
MAOI plus tramadol or SSRI without 2-week washout is a high-risk combination
explanation.pearls: Hunter Criteria for serotonin syndrome: spontaneous clonus, OR inducible clonus with agitation/diaphoresis, OR ocular clonus with agitation/diaphoresis, OR tremor and hyperreflexia, OR hypertonia plus T>38 with clonus. Lower limb dominance and mydriasis distinguish from NMS. MAOI plus tramadol is classic.

stem:
A 64-year-old man with bipolar I disorder on lithium for 15 years presents to the emergency department brought by his daughter with confusion, slurred speech, and tremor that has worsened over a week. She mentions he had a chest infection 10 days ago and was started on an unknown medication by a locum GP. He has remained on his lithium 750 mg twice daily.

On arrival he is drowsy and disoriented to time. He has coarse tremor in both hands, gait ataxia when assisted up, and brisk deep tendon reflexes. There is no fever and no focal neurology. His past medical history includes hypertension on perindopril and chronic kidney disease stage 3.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 64 /min, blood pressure 152/94 mmHg, respiratory rate 14 /min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: drowsy, dysarthric, coarse bilateral hand tremor, gait ataxia, brisk reflexes
data_table.Investigations: lithium level 3.4 mmol/L (0.4 to 1.0); creatinine 180 micromol/L (45 to 90, baseline 110); sodium 138 mmol/L; potassium 4.2 mmol/L; ECG: sinus bradycardia with flattened T waves

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g via a nasogastric tube now
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does not bind lithium and has no role in lithium toxicity. It is not recommended in any toxicology guideline for this indication. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 200 mL/hr alone and recheck the level in 6 hours
options.rationale: Saline alone is appropriate for mild toxicity in normal renal function but is insufficient in CKD with AKI, encephalopathy, and a level over 2.5 mmol/L. EXTRIP supports dialysis in this scenario. (Source: EXTRIP)

options.text: Arrange urgent haemodialysis after IV 0.9% sodium chloride is commenced
options.rationale: EXTRIP consensus recommends haemodialysis for lithium toxicity when the level exceeds 4.0 mmol/L, OR exceeds 2.5 mmol/L with significant impaired consciousness, seizures, life-threatening arrhythmia, OR exceeds 2.5 mmol/L with renal impairment that will impair clearance. This patient meets the third criterion: level 3.4 with CKD stage 3 and acute kidney injury, plus altered mental status. IV saline is started while dialysis is arranged. (Source: EXTRIP Workgroup Lithium Recommendations)

options.text: Withhold lithium and recheck levels in 24 hours without other intervention
options.rationale: Watchful waiting in a patient with encephalopathy, AKI, and a level of 3.4 mmol/L risks deterioration, seizures, and arrhythmia. Active clearance is needed. (Source: EXTRIP)

options.text: Administer sodium polystyrene sulfonate to lower the lithium level
options.rationale: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate has been investigated for lithium binding but is not endorsed as primary therapy. EXTRIP supports haemodialysis as the definitive extracorporeal removal modality. (Source: EXTRIP)

explanation.summary: Lithium toxicity occurs by reduced renal clearance (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, dehydration, AKI), dose error, or deliberate overdose. EXTRIP recommendations for haemodialysis are: any lithium level over 4.0 mmol/L, OR over 2.5 mmol/L with significant impaired consciousness, seizures, life-threatening arrhythmia, OR over 2.5 mmol/L with renal impairment that will prevent clearance within 36 hours. Saline IV at 1.5 to 2 times maintenance is given while dialysis is arranged. Rebound rises after dialysis (intracellular shifts) require recheck and may need re-dialysis. The classic clinical triad is coarse tremor, ataxia, and altered mental status.
key_points: Haemodialysis if level over 4.0 mmol/L, OR over 2.5 mmol/L with encephalopathy, seizures, arrhythmia, OR renal impairment
IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 1.5 to 2 times maintenance while dialysis is arranged
Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium
Expect rebound levels after dialysis from intracellular redistribution
explanation.pearls: Lithium toxicity dialysis (EXTRIP): level over 4.0 mmol/L, OR over 2.5 mmol/L with encephalopathy/seizures/arrhythmia, OR over 2.5 mmol/L with renal impairment. Saline IV at 1.5 to 2 times maintenance bridges to dialysis. Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium.

stem:
A 29-year-old engineer presents to your practice asking about adult ADHD assessment. He describes lifelong difficulty starting and finishing tasks, frequent forgetfulness, fidgeting through meetings, and a pattern of late submissions throughout university and at work. School reports from age 8 noted disorganisation and inattention; he completed his degree but struggled. He has no history of mood or psychotic disorder, no substance dependence, no head injury, and no medical illness on review.

His cardiovascular history is unremarkable. His resting pulse is 72 /min and BP 122/78 mmHg. He has no personal or family history of structural heart disease, sudden death under 40, or unexplained syncope. His full mental state and neurological examination today are unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 72 /min, blood pressure 122/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 14 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: no abnormal findings; cardiovascular examination unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: thyroid function, FBC, UEC all within normal limits; ECG sinus rhythm rate 72 with no abnormalities

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure adult-onset ADHD is rare and offer CBT for organisation skills only
options.rationale: ADHD is by definition a developmental disorder with symptoms from childhood, and adult presentation reflecting previously unrecognised lifelong difficulty is common. Dismissing the assessment misses a treatable condition. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Trial methylphenidate 10 mg twice daily in the practice and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: GPs cannot initiate stimulants without specialist authority under state Schedule 8 legislation. Initiation requires specialist diagnosis. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Commence atomoxetine 40 mg daily and titrate up to 80 mg over 2 weeks
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is non-stimulant but still requires authority initiation. Adult ADHD diagnosis must be specialist-confirmed before any first-line medication. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Refer for neuropsychological testing as the diagnostic gold standard
options.rationale: Neuropsychological testing supplements clinical assessment but is not the diagnostic standard. ADHD is a clinical diagnosis based on developmental history, current impairment across settings, and DSM-5-TR criteria. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Refer to a psychiatrist or paediatrician with stimulant authority for diagnosis
options.rationale: Adult ADHD diagnosis in Australia requires confirmation by an appropriately credentialed specialist (psychiatrist, paediatrician, or in some states neurologist) with stimulant prescribing authority. Stimulants are Schedule 8 and need state authority. GPs co-prescribe under a shared-care plan once initiated. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

explanation.summary: The 2022 Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD sets out a structured pathway: GP triage with collateral history and screening (ASRS), then specialist referral for diagnostic confirmation by a psychiatrist, paediatrician, or, in some states, neurologist with stimulant prescribing authority. Stimulant initiation (methylphenidate or dexamfetamine) requires Schedule 8 authority and ECG and cardiovascular screening before initiation. Driving counselling, comorbidity screening (anxiety, depression, substance use), and informed consent on diversion risks are required. GPs continue maintenance under shared-care arrangements.
key_points: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires specialist confirmation by a psychiatrist or paediatrician
Stimulants are Schedule 8 and need state authority before initiation
Cardiovascular screen (history and ECG) before stimulant initiation
GPs continue maintenance under shared-care arrangements
explanation.pearls: Adult ADHD: GP screens (ASRS) and refers; specialist (psychiatrist, paediatrician, sometimes neurologist) confirms diagnosis and initiates stimulant under Schedule 8 authority. CV screen and ECG before initiation; GP shared-care maintenance.

sources.label: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

stem:
A 34-year-old non-pregnant woman presents to the emergency department with two days of fevers, rigors, and right-sided loin pain on a background of dysuria and frequency for four days. She is otherwise well, has no allergies, has no urinary tract abnormalities, and is not catheterised. She is sexually active with a male partner and uses condoms; she had a uncomplicated cystitis 14 months ago treated with trimethoprim.

On examination she is unwell but not septic. There is marked right costovertebral angle tenderness. The abdomen is otherwise soft. She is tolerating oral fluids and food.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 102 /min, blood pressure 116/72 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 38.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: right costovertebral angle tenderness, soft abdomen, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: urine dipstick: leukocytes 3+, nitrites positive, blood 1+; FBC WCC 14.6 x10^9/L; CRP 88 mg/L; creatinine 78 micromol/L; lactate 1.8 mmol/L; urine culture and blood cultures sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic?

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7 days at home
options.rationale: Oral ciprofloxacin is reserved for mild pyelonephritis suitable for outpatient management. Significant fever, rigors, and raised inflammatory markers warrant initial IV therapy and observation under Therapeutic Guidelines. Fluoroquinolone resistance in E. coli is also a regional issue. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg once-daily plus IV amoxicillin 2 g six hourly
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic recommends IV gentamicin plus amoxicillin (or ampicillin) as first-line empirical therapy for moderate to severe acute pyelonephritis in non-pregnant adults requiring admission. Gentamicin covers gram-negative uropathogens including most E. coli; amoxicillin extends cover for enterococci. Step-down to oral guided by culture and sensitivity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 14 days as outpatient therapy
options.rationale: Trimethoprim is appropriate for uncomplicated lower UTI when local resistance is low. It does not provide adequate parenteral cover for pyelonephritis and oral monotherapy is insufficient at this severity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading then 15 mg/kg twelve hourly
options.rationale: Vancomycin targets gram-positive organisms (MRSA, enterococci) and does not cover the dominant gram-negative uropathogens. It is not an empirical choice for pyelonephritis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV meropenem 1 g eight hourly as the empirical first-line agent
options.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for severe sepsis with risk factors for multi-drug resistance, ESBL exposure, or failure of first-line. Routine first-line use drives carbapenem resistance and is not Therapeutic Guidelines first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Acute pyelonephritis in non-pregnant adults requiring admission is empirically treated with IV gentamicin (5 to 7 mg/kg once-daily, calculated by ideal body weight) plus IV amoxicillin 2 g six hourly per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic. Mild pyelonephritis suitable for outpatient management can be treated with oral ciprofloxacin or amoxicillin-clavulanate guided by local resistance. Always send urine and blood cultures before antibiotics. Step-down to a culture-directed oral agent for a total 10 to 14 day course. Consider imaging (CT or US) if no improvement at 48 to 72 hours or if anatomic abnormalities are suspected.
key_points: Moderate to severe pyelonephritis: IV gentamicin once-daily plus IV amoxicillin first-line
Mild outpatient pyelonephritis: oral ciprofloxacin or amoxicillin-clavulanate
Always urine and blood cultures before antibiotics; step-down to oral by sensitivity
Imaging at 48 to 72 hours if no improvement to exclude complications
explanation.pearls: Adult acute pyelonephritis (admission): IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg once-daily plus IV amoxicillin 2 g six hourly per Therapeutic Guidelines. Step-down to culture-directed oral for total 10 to 14 days. Image if no improvement at 48 to 72 hours.

stem:
A 68-year-old man with COPD reviews at your respiratory clinic. He has a 50 pack-year history (quit 4 years ago), GOLD group B disease, FEV1 56% predicted post-bronchodilator, and has been on tiotropium 18 micrograms daily for two years. In the past year he has had three moderate exacerbations requiring oral prednisolone and antibiotics, none requiring hospital admission. His mMRC dyspnoea grade is 3.

His blood eosinophil count is 380 cells per microlitre on a recent FBC. He has had no pneumonias. He uses his short-acting salbutamol inhaler about four times a week. He is on optimal non-pharmacological therapy (pulmonary rehabilitation completed, influenza and pneumococcal vaccines up to date, smoking cessation maintained).

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 84 /min, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 /min, SpO2 95% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: barrel chest, reduced air entry bilaterally, no wheeze at rest, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: FEV1/FVC 0.58 post-bronchodilator; FEV1 56% predicted; blood eosinophils 380 cells per microlitre

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in inhaled therapy?

options.text: Add oral azithromycin 250 mg three times weekly as long-term prophylaxis
options.rationale: Long-term macrolide prophylaxis is considered for frequent exacerbators on optimal inhaled therapy including triple inhaler; it is not the next step before escalating inhalers. Cardiac and hearing screening are required. (Source: GOLD 2024; COPD-X)

options.text: Add a LABA to the LAMA but defer ICS until further exacerbations
options.rationale: LAMA plus LABA (no ICS) is appropriate if eosinophils are below 100 cells per microlitre and exacerbations are infrequent. With eosinophils 380 and three exacerbations in a year, ICS is indicated under GOLD and COPD-X. (Source: GOLD 2024; COPD-X)

options.text: Escalate to a LAMA plus LABA plus inhaled corticosteroid triple inhaler
options.rationale: GOLD 2024 and Lung Foundation Australia recommend escalating to triple therapy (LAMA plus LABA plus ICS) for patients with two or more moderate exacerbations or one hospitalisation per year despite LAMA monotherapy, particularly when blood eosinophils are 300 cells per microlitre or higher. ICS efficacy is greatest in this eosinophilic phenotype. (Source: GOLD 2024 Strategy; COPD-X Australian Guidelines)

options.text: Add oral theophylline 200 mg twice daily and review symptoms in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Theophylline has a narrow therapeutic window and modest bronchodilator effect; it is reserved for refractory cases when inhaled options are exhausted. (Source: GOLD 2024; COPD-X)

options.text: Stop tiotropium and switch to inhaled corticosteroid monotherapy
options.rationale: ICS monotherapy in COPD is inferior to LAMA monotherapy and increases pneumonia risk. Stopping the LAMA is harmful. (Source: GOLD 2024; COPD-X)

explanation.summary: GOLD 2024 and Lung Foundation Australia's COPD-X guideline use exacerbation history and eosinophil count to direct ICS use. Patients with two or more moderate exacerbations or one hospitalisation per year on LAMA or LABA monotherapy should escalate to LAMA plus LABA, with ICS added if eosinophils are 300 cells per microlitre or higher (or 100 to 300 with frequent exacerbations). Triple therapy in a single inhaler improves adherence. After 3 to 6 months of triple therapy, ICS can be considered for withdrawal if no exacerbations and eosinophils remain low; long-term azithromycin is added for ongoing frequent exacerbators despite triple therapy.
key_points: Two or more moderate exacerbations per year on LAMA: escalate to LAMA plus LABA plus ICS if eosinophils 300 or higher
ICS benefit is greatest at eosinophils 300 cells per microlitre or higher
Long-term azithromycin reserved for frequent exacerbators despite triple inhaler
ICS monotherapy is not used in COPD; pneumonia risk and inferior outcomes
explanation.pearls: GOLD/COPD-X COPD escalation: two or more exacerbations on LAMA, escalate to LAMA+LABA+ICS especially if eosinophils 300 cells per microlitre or higher. Long-term azithromycin only for frequent exacerbators on triple therapy. ICS monotherapy is not used in COPD.
